serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw macadamia nuts is approximately 30g (about 10–12 whole nuts), providing roughly 215 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Macadamia nuts, raw — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw macadamia nuts (cr_id F09030033). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry for macadamia nuts, raw (FDC ID 170178) for secondary validation and to supplement missing micronutrient data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Macadamia nuts are a very low-carbohydrate, high-fat nut. GI of approximately 10 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database category for mixed nuts and tree nuts, consistent with peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (10 × 13.82 × 0.30) / 100 ≈ 0.41, rounded to 0.4. The very low GI reflects the high fat and fibre content that substantially blunts any glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100g), high potassium (368 mg/100g), MUFA-dominant fat profile supporting endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Raw macadamia nuts are well-suited for hypertension management: their very low sodium content (5 mg/100g) and high potassium (368 mg/100g) align with DASH diet principles, and the dominant monounsaturated fat (oleic acid ~58.9g/100g) supports arterial elasticity; limit to ~30g per day due to high caloric density (718 kcal/100g) and choose unsalted raw variants only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~10), high dietary fibre (8.6g/100g), high MUFA content improving insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw macadamia nuts have a very low glycemic index (~10) and high dietary fibre (8.6g/100g) that slows gastric emptying and attenuates postprandial glucose excursions, making them appropriate for blood glucose management per ADA guidelines; restrict to ~30g per day to manage caloric intake and avoid honey-roasted or sugar-coated variants.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA content (oleic acid ~58.9g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (8.6g/100g), low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw macadamia nuts have a cardioprotective lipid profile dominated by monounsaturated oleic acid (~58.9g/100g), zero trans fat, and zero cholesterol, with high dietary fibre (8.6g/100g) supporting LDL reduction; saturated fat is moderate at 12.1g/100g and intake should be limited to ~30g per day within a heart-healthy dietary pattern consistent with AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA (anti-inflammatory oleic acid), high fibre, vitamin E and polyphenols; saturated fat at 12.1g/100g marginally exceeds conservative RA thresholds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw macadamia nuts provide anti-inflammatory benefit through dominant MUFA (oleic acid ~58.9g/100g), high fibre (8.6g/100g), and vitamin E (0.54mg/100g), but saturated fat at ~12.1g/100g marginally exceeds conservative RA dietary thresholds; limit to ~30g per day and choose raw unsalted variants to minimise joint-load risk from excess caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw macadamia nuts (Macadamia integrifolia / Macadamia tetraphylla) are whole, unprocessed tree nuts native to Australia and widely cultivated in Hawaii, South Africa, and other subtropical regions. They are cream to ivory in colour, spherical, with a firm yet buttery-smooth texture and a distinctively rich, sweet, and mildly buttery flavour that sets them apart from other tree nuts. They are typically consumed raw as a snack, but can also be lightly roasted to enhance their flavour. They are a versatile ingredient used in baking, confectionery, nut butters, and as a premium addition to salads and trail mixes. Nutritionally, raw macadamia nuts are one of the highest-fat nuts available, with 75.77g fat per 100g, dominated by monounsaturated oleic acid (~58.9g/100g), which is associated with cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. They provide 718 kcal/100g, 7.91g protein, 13.82g carbohydrate, 8.6g dietary fibre, and only 5mg sodium per 100g. Their very low glycemic index (~10) and high fibre content make them suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio (368mg:5mg) aligns with DASH dietary principles for hypertension. They are naturally cholesterol-free, trans-fat-free, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Clinically, they are recommended in small portions (~30g/day) due to high caloric density. They are a tree nut allergen and must be labelled accordingly.
common_user_queries: Are macadamia nuts good for diabetics?
Can I eat macadamia nuts if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in macadamia nuts?
What is the glycemic index of macadamia nuts?
How much fat is in macadamia nuts?
Are macadamia nuts high in fibre?
What are the health benefits of raw macadamia nuts?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09030033, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source, with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170178) for macadamia nuts raw. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are consistent between both approved sources. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database category data for tree nuts, consistent with peer-reviewed literature.
notes: Saturated fat value of 12.06g/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170178) as HPB record did not separately report saturated fat breakdown; this is consistent with known macadamia fatty acid profiles. Monounsaturated fat (58.88g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.50g/100g) also sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170178. Trans_fat_g is confirmed as 0.0g per USDA FoodData Central. Vitamin and mineral micronutrient values (vitamin C, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, B12, folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170178 as HPB record did not report these fields. Vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero in plant foods; vitamin_d_mcg is naturally zero in raw macadamia nuts per USDA data; vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 per USDA data. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA sources. GI of ~10 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database tree nut category data; no direct published GI test for raw macadamia nuts was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic load per 30g serving = (10 × 4.15) / 100 ≈ 0.4. The rheumatoid arthritis health context notes that saturated fat at 12.1g/100g marginally exceeds the conservative MUFA/PUFA carve-out ceiling of 12g/100g; suitable flag set to moderate concern rather than a binary unsuitable. dietary_tags 'wholegrain' removed from output as macadamia nuts are not a grain product; all other input dietary tags preserved. No ontology codes were invented; FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches for raw macadamia nut.
